title: Passenger's Experience in Automated Vehicles: Overview of Electrogastrography Application for Car Sickness Assessment
author: Miljković, Nadica
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Presentation for the Workshop motion comfort of automated driving held online on September 1, 2021, before the International Comfort Congress (ICC,  https://comfort.ergonomics.org.uk/programme ). 

 Video recording for the presentation is available on  YouTube . 

 ABSTRACT 

 Automation of driving functions in modern vehicles liberate drivers from the primary task of constantly controlling the vehicle and monitoring the road and therefore provide more spare time during the drive and possibility to engage in a variety of secondary non-driving related tasks. However, this benefit sometimes comes with the cost – motion sickness and nausea caused by unexpected vehicle maneuvers and the conflicts among senses. In order to provide more comfortable passenger’s experience, the car sickness sensation should be eliminated at best or decreased at least. For a successful mitigation of sickness symptoms, in-depth investigation of its causes and manifestations is required. 

 In this talk, we will explore a non-invasive and easy-to-use technique to record electrical potentials originating from stomach smooth muscles – electrogastrography (EGG) and its application for sickness assessment. EGG efficacy in monitoring dysrhythmical stomach activity commonly associated with the sensation of nausea has been previously reported with some success. The main obstacle in wider EGG application is its susceptibility to artifacts and especially the ones associated with subject’s movements. Therefore, appropriate precautions should be taken in order to record and process EGG properly. Practical considerations with experiences related to protocol design, electrode placement, recording duration, pre-processing, and feature extraction will be presented. 

 Driving simulators provide attractive frameworks for conducting safe and efficient automated drive testing procedures. We will present results of a study conducted in a driving simulator with the aim to indicate short-term and long-term EGG features for sickness assessment in relation to the driving environment. 

 In the end, a discussion on possible future directions for EGG-based applications for assessment of car sickness will be conducted. The potential advancements include and are not limited to the: less obtrusive procedures, advanced processing methods for noise elimination, multiple signal extraction from single channel recording, application of other sensing principles etc. Upcoming research and development directions will be explored.
